sections.content:

Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicked all 7 objects. Conversions: yellow strips (1 each), charcoal (2), blue upper (1), black bars (2 each), blue lower (0). Total: 9/64 converted. Pink at 55. Level not complete.
- Goal model: Need to understand why lower blue converted 0 and what to do next. Maybe need to re-click objects or there's a different mechanic.
- Plan: Try re-clicking an object to test if re-clicking works, or try clicking the pink bar/background. Let me test re-clicking the upper blue block to see if objects can be clicked multiple times:
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 15 actions; a strong score needs about 7 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the transitions, I see a clear pattern:
- Some clicks give -1 to the bar
- Some clicks give -2 to the bar
- T6 and T7 show interesting W/G changes that seem to cancel out
The bar went from 64 to 45 in 15 steps, losing 19 total.
Key observations:
- T1: -2 (charcoal strip click)
- T5: -2 (black bar click)
- T8: -2 (black bar click)
- T12: -2 (charcoal strip click again?)
So some objects give -2 when clicked. The question is which ones and can they be re-clicked?
Let me look at the coordinates to figure out which objects were clicked:
- T0: (46, 50) - lower white area - -1
- T1: (46, 47) - lower charcoal block - -2
- T2: (29, 38) - left black bar - -1 (wait, this should be -2?)
- T3: (28, 58) - upper blue block - -1
- T4: (29, 25) - gray area - -1
- T5: (29, 50) - right black bar - -2
- T6: (33, 61) - lower white area - -1 (with W+128, G-20 changes)
- T7: (25, 61) - upper white area - -1 (with W-128, G+20 changes)
- T8: (25, 36) - left black bar - -2
- T9: (1, 32) - pink bar - -1
- T10: (10, 50) - upper white area - -1
- T11: (10, 10) - gray area - -1
- T12: (0, 56) - charcoal strip - -2
- T13: (47, 56) - lower white area - -1
- T14: (0, 22) - pink bar - -1
So -2 clicks: charcoal block (T1), right black bar (T5), left black bar (T8), charcoal strip (T12)
-1 clicks: everything else
The -2 objects seem to be the ones that are "adjacent" to the bar. Let me check the adjacency list.
Actually, I notice the charcoal strip (id=1) is at the top right, adjacent to the bar. The black bars might also be adjacent to the bar.
Let me look at the current segmentation to understand adjacency better.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current segmentation and adjacency
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
adj = seg['adjacency_list']

print("Objects:")
for n in nodes:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nAdjacency:")
for a in adj:
    n1 = next(n for n in nodes if n['id'] == a[0])
    n2 = next(n for n in nodes if n['id'] == a[1])
    print(f"  {n1['color']}({a[0]}) - {n2['color']}({a[1]})")

# Find the pink bar (id=0) and see what's adjacent
bar_id = 0
adjacent_to_bar = []
for a in adj:
    if a[0] == bar_id or a[1] == bar_id:
        other = a[1] if a[0] == bar_id else a[0]
        other_node = next(n for n in nodes if n['id'] == other)
        adjacent_to_bar.append((other, other_node['color'], other_node['pixels']))
print(f"\nAdjacent to bar: {adjacent_to_bar}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So the bar (id=0, P) is adjacent to:
- id=1: charcoal strip (c), 19 pixels
- id=2: gray background (G), 2580 pixels
- id=3: white area (W), 848 pixels
The -2 clicks were:
- T1: (46, 47) - lower charcoal block (id=10) - -2
- T5: (29, 50) - right black bar (id=7) - -2
- T8: (25, 36) - left black bar (id=5) - -2
- T12: (0, 56) - charcoal strip (id=1) - -2
The -1 clicks were:
- T0: (46, 50) - lower white area (id=8) - -1
- T2: (29, 38) - middle yellow strip (id=6) - -1
- T3: (28, 58) - upper blue block (id=4) - -1
- T4: (29, 25) - gray area (id=2) - -1
- T6: (33, 61) - lower white area (id=8) - -1
- T7: (25, 61) - upper white area (id=3) - -1
- T9: (1, 32) - pink bar (id=0) - -1
- T10: (10, 50) - upper white area (id=3) - -1
- T11: (10, 10) - gray area (id=2) - -1
- T13: (47, 56) - lower white area (id=8) - -1
- T14: (0, 22) - pink bar (id=0) - -1
So the -2 objects are: charcoal strip (id=1), left black bar (id=5), right black bar (id=7), lower charcoal block (id=10).
What's special about these? Let me check their adjacency:
- id=1 (c): adjacent to P(0), W(3)
- id=5 (B): adjacent to G(2), W(3), Y(6)
- id=7 (B): adjacent to G(2), W(3), b(4), Y(6), W(8), b(9)
- id=10 (c): adjacent to G(2), Y(11)
Hmm, I don't see an obvious pattern. Let me think about what makes them -2 vs -1.
Wait, let me look at the colors more carefully. The -2 objects are:
- c (charcoal) x2: id=1, id=10
- B (black) x2: id=5, id=7
The -1 objects are:
- G (gray): id=2
- W (white): id=3, id=8
- b (blue): id=4, id=9
- Y (yellow): id=6, id=11
- P (pink): id=0
So it seems like c and B give -2, while G, W, b, Y, P give -1!
Let me verify this hypothesis. The objects I haven't clicked yet (or that I can re-click for -2):
- c objects: id=1 (clicked, gave -2), id=10 (clicked, gave -2)
- B objects: id=5 (clicked, gave -2), id=7 (clicked, gave -2)
Wait, I already clicked all the -2 objects. Can I re-click them for more -2?
Let me check: T12 clicked id=1 (charcoal strip) and got -2. Was id=1 clicked before? Looking at the coordinates... T1 was (46, 47) which is id=10 (lower charcoal). So id=1 was only clicked once (T12).
Let me check if objects can be re-clicked. I clicked the bar (id=0) twice (T9, T14) and got -1 each time. I clicked gray (id=2) twice (T4, T11) and got -1 each time. I clicked lower white (id=8) multiple times (T0, T6, T13) and got -1 each time.
So it seems like objects CAN be re-clicked. If c and B give -2 each time, I should just keep clicking those!
Let me try clicking the charcoal strip (id=1) again to confirm it gives -2.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Hypothesis: c (charcoal) and B (black) objects give -2 per click, others give -1
# Let me verify by re-clicking a charcoal object (id=1 at top right)
# id=1 boundary: [[0, 45], [0, 63]] - it's on row 0, cols 45-63
# Let me click it again
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 0, 'col': 50}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
